items.question: Who uses DBSCAN in practice?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers on geospatial analytics, anomaly screening, and exploratory segmentation; GIS analysts for hotspot detection; and robotics teams grouping LiDAR patches when cluster shape is irregular and outliers must be flagged explicitly.

items.question: What does DBSCAN optimize or define?
items.answer: It defines clusters as maximal sets of density-connected points: regions where core points (≥ min_samples neighbors within eps) chain through eps-neighborhoods; points not connected are labeled noise rather than assigned to arbitrary groups.

items.question: When should you choose DBSCAN over k-means?
items.answer: When cluster count is unknown, shapes are non-spherical, outliers should remain unclustered, or density structure matters more than centroid-based variance minimization — after scaling features and planning eps tuning.

items.question: Where in the ML workflow does DBSCAN run?
items.answer: On unlabeled feature matrix X after preprocessing (scaling, optional dimensionality reduction): fit_predict assigns cluster IDs and noise label −1; results are validated with Cluster Evaluation and domain inspection before downstream use.

items.question: Why do eps and min_samples interact?
items.answer: eps sets neighborhood radius while min_samples sets how many neighbors define a dense core — larger eps merges more points; higher min_samples demands tighter local density before a region can seed cluster growth, often increasing noise labels.

items.question: How do you tune DBSCAN hyperparameters?
items.answer: Scale features, pick min_samples relative to dimensionality (often ≥ d+1), plot k-distance (k = min_samples−1) for eps elbow, fit DBSCAN, then check noise fraction, cluster sizes, stability, and metrics from Cluster Evaluation on non-noise points.

items.question: DBSCAN vs k-means?
items.answer: DBSCAN discovers density-connected clusters without fixing k and labels noise; k-means partitions all points into k spherical groups around centroids. DBSCAN suits irregular shapes and outliers; k-means suits similar-sized blobs when k is known or tuned separately.

items.question: Core point vs border point vs noise?
items.answer: Core: ≥ min_samples neighbors within eps — seeds expansion. Border: within eps of a core but not core itself — in cluster, no expansion. Noise: neither — label −1. k-means has no analogous noise class; every point belongs to some cluster.

items.question: If DBSCAN does not take k, what controls how many clusters appear?
items.answer: eps and min_samples jointly define which points are dense enough to connect — smaller eps or higher min_samples tend to split or eliminate clusters; larger eps merges density-connected components into fewer groups.

items.question: Why can a visually nearby point still be labeled noise?
items.answer: Membership requires density reachability through core points — if the point has fewer than min_samples neighbors within eps and is not within eps of any core point, it is noise even if it looks close on a 2D plot due to other dimensions or sparse local density.

items.question: What breaks if you double eps while holding min_samples fixed?
items.answer: Neighborhoods overlap more — more points become core, chains connect farther apart, clusters merge, and noise fraction typically drops until one dominant component absorbs most of the dataset.

items.question: Cause: increase min_samples from 5 to 20 with fixed eps on scaled data. Effect?
items.answer: Fewer points qualify as cores, cluster seeds shrink, density chains break apart, cluster count often drops, and the fraction of noise labels (−1) usually increases.

items.question: Cause: run DBSCAN without feature scaling on mixed-scale tabular data. Effect?
items.answer: Distance is dominated by large-magnitude columns, eps-neighbor counts reflect scale artifacts not semantics, clusters align with arbitrary units, and tuning eps on a k-distance plot misleads because geometry is distorted.

items.question: What if eps is set larger than the maximum pairwise distance in the dataset?
items.answer: Every point lies in every other point's eps-neighborhood — if min_samples ≤ n, all points can become core and merge into a single density-connected cluster with zero or minimal noise.

items.question: What breaks DBSCAN when clusters have very different densities?
items.answer: A single global eps cannot simultaneously capture a tight dense cluster (needs small eps) and a sparse cluster (needs large eps) — one group merges into another or the sparse group is entirely labeled noise.

items.question: Central concept: DBSCAN
items.answer: Density-based unsupervised clustering: groups density-connected points using eps neighborhoods and min_samples core threshold; labels sparse points as noise.

items.question: Branch: hyperparameters
items.answer: eps (neighborhood radius) and min_samples (core density threshold) — tuned via k-distance plots and validated with Cluster Evaluation.

items.question: Branch: point types
items.answer: Core seeds clusters; border attaches without expanding; noise (−1) is not assigned to any cluster.

items.question: Branch: prerequisites
items.answer: Scaled features and distance metric from Distance Metrics; unsupervised framing from Unsupervised Learning; contrast with k-Means for fixed-k partitioning.
